raw_case:
Elevation of serum neurofilament light-chain levels disclose possible occult progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy and immune reconstitution syndrome in a patient receiving ozanimod: a case report


Background

We report the first case of findings suggestive of progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy and immune restitution syndrome (PML-IRIS) in a patient with multiple sclerosis receiving ozanimod preceded by an unexpected increase in the serum neurofilament light-chain (sNfL) levels.

Case report

A 57-year-old-woman treated with ozanimod for the last 8 years presented, during surveillance MRI, with findings compatible with PML-IRIS. Overt clinical symptoms were absent. sNfL levels increased 4 months earlier and peaked at presentation. Lymphocyte count reached nadir of 330/mL at 8 months earlier.

Conclusion

The case illustrates the utility of sNfL levels for PML surveillance in patients receiving immunosuppressors.

Background

Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) is a severe destructive opportunistic infection of the central nervous system characterized by rapid progression of neurological disability due to axonal destruction. Serum neurofilament light chain (sNfL), a biomarker of neuroaxonal injury, has shown to elevate to very high levels in this setting.Ozanimod is an oral spingosine-1-receptor (S1PR) modulator used to treat relapsing forms of multiple sclerosis (MS) and ulcerative colitis. As other disease-modifying treatments (DMT) for MS, S1PR modulators have been associated with infections, including PML. The drug binds to S1PR on the surface of lymphocytes, preventing egression from lymph nodes and, thus, reducing migration of inflammatory cells to the central nervous system. Since first clinical trials and approval in 2020, the manufacturer estimates an exposition of 40,000 patient-years wordwide as of April 2023. So far, only one case of PML has been reported (data available by Bristol-Myers Squibb) (2).The case presented herein is, to our knowledge, the first case of a possible PML simultaneous with immune restitution syndrome (PML-IRIS) in a patient ongoing treatment with ozanimod, which was disclosed by an unexpected elevation of sNfL levels.

Case report

A 57-year-old woman with relapsing remitting MS treated with ozanimod for the last 8 years presented, during routine follow-up MRI for surveillance due to marked sNfL level elevation, with findings suggestive of PML and IRIS (
Figure 1
). Her medical history was relevant for hypertension and depression. Five months prior, she suffered from mild SARS-CoV-2 infection with systemic and upper respiratory tract symptoms. She received ozanimod as her first DMT after diagnosis 10 years before, initially as part of a clinical trial, and then during routine clinical practice. Ever since, her disease had remained stable with no changes in expanded disability status scale (EDSS) and no signs of activity in yearly brain MRIs. Her baseline EDSS was 2.5, and her neurological exam was remarkable for sensory loss in her left arm, hyperreflexia, and mild decrease of mentation. In retrospect, the patient referred worsening of mentation months prior that could not be confirmed on neurological examination. At the time of MRI scan showing findings suggestive of PML, she had received ozanimod uninterruptedly for 115 months. The patient was admitted for rapid workup. Commercial real-time polymerase chain reaction (PCR; JCV ELITe MGB® Kit, ELITechGroup S.p.A, Torino, Italy) of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) was positive at 34 copies/mL, which was under the limit of the detection of the technique, and, therefore, was rendered negative by the laboratory. Repeated lumbar puncture 2 weeks later was also negative for JCV. CSF cytobiochemistry was unremarkable (glucose, 53 mg/dL; proteins, 34.8 mg/dL; and 3 leukocytes/μL). HIV serology was negative. Her absolute lymphocyte count (ALC) was 515/μL, 500/μL, and 330/μL (nadir) at presentation, 4 and 8 months, respectively, prior to MRI findings. She had performed ALC every 4 months, and all ALC ranged between 330 and 850 during the last 3 years. The remaining cell blood count and extensive biochemistry, including Immunoglobulin G (IgG), Immunoglobulin M (IgM), and Immunoglobulin A (IgA) levels, were within normal range.sNfL levels were determined every 4 months as part of clinical routine. Baseline sNfL levels in the year prior ranged between 8.5 pg/mL and 9.5 pg/mL but, 4 months before her levels rose to 47.9 pg/mL, peaked at 113.7 pg/mL at presentation and then descended to 43.4 pg/mL 2 weeks later. Retrospective revision of yearly brain MRIs confirmed no previous lesions suggestive of PML. A timeline of events of sNfL and ALC is shown in 
Figure 2
.Upon admission, the patient discontinued ozanimod and started treatment with oral mirtazapine 15 mg daily and a single dose of intravenous cidofovir 5 mg/kg body weight. Follow-up MRI scan performed 2 weeks later did not show significant changes. Subsequent MRI scan performed 24 days later showed, for the first time, absence of new gadolinium, enhancing lesions and similar T2/Flair lesions. Four months later, MRI shows no changes in T2/FLAIR lesions, resolution of restricted diffusion foci, and persistence of punctate gadolinium enhancement. She remains clinically stable, and the neurological exam has not shown any significant changes. Alternative therapy with fortnightly IM pegylated interferon β was initiated.

Discussion

We describe a 57-year-old woman treated with ozanimod for 10 years, without other known risk factors such as prior immunosuppression or exposure to other DMTs, who presented with findings suggestive of PML-IRIS detected brain MRI performed after an unexpected rise in sNfL levels. Despite highly suggestive MRI findings and compatible clinical presentation, PCR positivity was below the limit of detection of the technique, and, therefore, the presence of JC virus DNA in CSF could not be confirmed. Hence, we cannot rule out completely breakthrough MS radiographic activity, although we point out that this is very unlikely given the radiographic appearance and extreme elevation of sNfL levels.Asymptomatic or paucisymptomatic PML are increasingly recognized with growing number of patients treated with DMTs. Several MRI findings such as the presence punctate lesions in the vicinity of a new lesion (a.k.a. milky way sign, as seen in 
Figure 1
) have proven extremely specific of PML (3). The presence of IRIS in this setting further increases specificity to the diagnosis. In this circumstance, MRI is the cornerstone of diagnosis, as several MRI findings have been proved extremely specific of PML (see 
Figure 1
), such as the presence punctate lesions in the vicinity of a new lesion (a.k.a. milky way sign) (3). Our case also presented small punctate Gd enhancement within and outside of the main lesions, which is thought to correlate with histological findings of T- and B-cell infiltration due to partial immune reconstitution within perivascular spaces (4, 5). The fact that the patient presented with PML while ongoing treatment with ozanimod may explain the presence of partial IRIS. The patient also experienced an elevation of sNfL (a biomarker of axonal injury) from 9.5 pg/mL at baseline to 5-fold 4 months prior and to 12-fold at the time of diagnosis. This is in accordance with previous studies that show that sNfL increases more than 10-fold during PML and until IRIS onset. Such extremely high levels of sNfL are rarely seen are in MS and have shown to discriminate between PML and relapses. Such extremely high levels of sNfL are rarely seen are in MS and have shown to discriminate between PML and relapses (1, 6). However, PCR of CSF may yield false-negative results, especially in mild cases or when IRIS is present. Thus, a negative PCR in this setting should not discard PML. In such cases, biopsy becomes critical to establish diagnosis. Because our patient had minimal symptoms, the risk-benefit ratio of performing a biopsy was considered unfavorable. However, according to proposed criteria, diagnosis of PML cannot be established in an asymptomatic patient without a positive PCR in CSF or biopsy, leading to no diagnosis despite that it may evolve to a potentially lethal condition (7). In light of current biomarkers, we propose that criteria should be revised to include the presence of highly specific MRI findings accompanied by extreme sNfL elevation in the category of “possible PML”.This report represents the first case of possible PML-IRIS and the second case of PML while on treatment with ozanimod, after a 46-year-old woman with symptomatic PML following treatment for 4 years, treated previously with interferon β-1a and an ALC of 500/μL (2). Our case differs in the paucity of symptoms and that it presented simultaneously with signs of IRIS (PML-IRIS) despite ongoing treatment. Although IRIS occurs usually weeks or months after cessation of the immunosuppressive treatment, another case of PML-IRIS while ongoing treatment with a S1PR modulator has also been reported (8). Months prior to presentation, our patient also suffered of mild SARS-CoV-2 infection, which has been related to PML infection too (9, 10), although the causality of these events remains speculative. The case reminds the necessity of surveillance for PML in patients with newer S1PR modulators, bearing in mind that overt clinical findings may not be present. The utility of sNfL should be underscored as our patient exhibited a striking increase in sNfL months prior to MRI findings allowing for a prompt diagnosis.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 57-year-old woman with a 10-year history of relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis treated with ozanimod for 8 years.
- Chief Complaint: None (asymptomatic with no overt clinical findings).
- History of Present Illness: Possible PML-IRIS detected during surveillance imaging after an elevation in serum neurofilament light-chain (sNfL) levels. The patient had a history of recently worsening mentation months before presentation but no clear neurological deficits during examination.
- Past Medical History: Hypertension, depression, and SARS-CoV-2 infection 5 months prior to presentation.
- Personal History: None.
- Family History: None.
- Physical Examination: Baseline neurological exam was remarkable only for sensory loss in the left arm, hyperreflexia, and mild decrease in mentation. No significant changes were noted upon MRI detection of lesions suggestive of PML-IRIS.
- Ancillary Tests: 
  - MRI: Findings suggestive of PML-IRIS (punctate gadolinium-enhancing lesions, consistent with the "milky way sign").
  - JC virus (JCV) PCR in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF): Positive with low viral copies (34 copies/mL); subsequent negative finding on repeat lumbar puncture.
  - CSF cytobiochemistry: Normal (glucose, proteins, and cell counts).
  - Serum neurofilament light-chain (sNfL): Elevated from baseline (8.5–9.5 pg/mL) to 47.9 pg/mL (4 months prior) and 113.7 pg/mL (peak at presentation).
  - Absolute lymphocyte count: Persistent lymphopenia (range: 330–850 cells/μL over 3 years), with a nadir of 330 cells/µL 8 months prior to presentation.
  - Other labs: Normal immune globulins, negative HIV serology, and normal remaining blood work.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Multiple sclerosis (MS) activity flare-up:**
   - Considered because the patient has relapsing-remitting MS but excluded based on the absence of typical MRI findings for MS flares and abnormally high sNfL levels, which are rarely seen in MS relapses.

2. **Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML):**
   - Strongly suspected due to MRI findings (e.g., the "milky way sign"), lymphopenia, and an extreme elevation of sNfL. However, definitive diagnosis was limited by the negative JCV PCR in CSF, as per current diagnostic criteria.

3. **PML with immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (PML-IRIS):**
   - Favored due to the absence of new gadolinium-enhancing lesions in follow-up MRIs, the presence of punctate gadolinium enhancements surrounding lesions (hallmark of IRIS), and continuing ozanimod treatment possibly leading to partial immune reconstitution. SARS-CoV-2 infection months prior may also have contributed.

4. **Other opportunistic infections or malignancies:**
   - Lesser likelihood due to normal metabolic and immune workup, MRI findings specific to PML, and absence of systemic symptoms.

5. **Alternative CNS conditions (e.g., vasculitis, other white matter diseases):**
   - Excluded based on MRI, CSF findings, and the clinical course being inconsistent with these conditions.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: "Possible progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy with immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (PML-IRIS)" in a patient treated with ozanimod for multiple sclerosis.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient's history. She's a 57-year-old woman with a 10-year history of relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (RRMS) treated with ozanimod for 8 years. Ozanimod is a sphingosine-1-phosphate (S1P) receptor modulator, which I remember can increase the risk of infections due to lymphopenia. That's important because PML is a concern in immunocompromised patients, especially those on certain immunosuppressive therapies.

Her chief complaint is asymptomatic, but the reason for investigation was elevated sNfL levels. sNfL is a biomarker for neuroaxonal damage, so a significant increase might indicate active demyelination or another process like PML. She had worsening mentation months prior but no clear deficits on exam. That's a bit conflicting because PML can present with neuropsychiatric symptoms, but her exam didn't show overt deficits.

Looking at her past medical history: hypertension, depression, and COVID-19 five months ago. COVID could be a red herring, but I don't think it's directly related unless there's a post-infectious complication. Her PMH doesn't add much else except maybe the depression, which can sometimes complicate cognitive assessments.

Physical exam findings: sensory loss in left arm (common in MS), hyperreflexia (also MS-related), and mild decreased mentation. But no significant changes when PML-IRIS was detected on MRI. Wait, PML-IRIS typically occurs when the immune system starts recovering and causes inflammation around the JC virus-infected areas. So the MRI findings here are crucial.

Ancillary tests: MRI shows punctate gadolinium-enhancing lesions, the "milky way sign" which is associated with PML-IRIS. That's a key point. The JC virus PCR in CSF was positive initially with low copies (34 copies/mL) but negative on repeat. Low viral load might explain the negative repeat if the sample was taken after starting treatment or if it's fluctuating. CSF cytobiochemistry is normal, which is typical in PML unless there's IRIS, which can cause pleocytosis. But here, cell counts are normal, which might support PML without significant inflammation, but the MRI suggests IRIS.

sNfL levels are elevated from baseline 8.5–9.5 pg/mL to 47.9 and then 113.7. That's a big jump, which could indicate active neuronal damage, like PML progression or IRIS. Absolute lymphocyte count is persistently low (330–850 cells/μL) with a nadir of 330. Ozanimod is known to cause lymphopenia by sequestering lymphocytes in lymph nodes. Persistent lymphopenia increases the risk of opportunistic infections like PML.

Other labs: normal immunoglobulins, negative HIV, which rules out other causes of immunodeficiency. So the main risk factor here is the ozanimod-induced lymphopenia.

Putting this together: The patient is on ozanimod, has lymphopenia, elevated sNfL, and MRI findings consistent with PML-IRIS. The initial positive JCV PCR in CSF supports PML, even though the repeat was negative. False negatives can happen, especially if the viral load is low. The combination of imaging findings, clinical context, and JCV positivity (even once) points towards PML-IRIS.

But wait, PML itself is a diagnosis based on clinical, imaging, and JCV in CSF. IRIS occurs when immune reconstitution leads to inflammation around PML lesions. In this case, the patient's lymphocyte count is still low, so how does IRIS happen? Maybe the immune system isn't fully reconstituted but starts reacting. Alternatively, could the MRI findings be mistaken for PML-IRIS? The "milky way sign" with punctate enhancements is pretty specific. Also, the sNfL peak at presentation correlates with active damage, which could be from IRIS-driven inflammation.

Alternative possibilities: Could this be an MS relapse? But she's on ozanimod, which should reduce relapses. The MRI findings of PML-IRIS are different from typical MS lesions. Also, sNfL is much higher than typical MS activity. Another thought: COVID-related complications? But timing is 5 months prior, and no clear link here.

So, the most likely diagnosis is PML-IRIS in the setting of ozanimod therapy for MS. The key factors are the MRI signs, JCV positivity in CSF (even transient), lymphopenia, and elevated sNfL. Even though she's asymptomatic now, the history of worsening mentation and imaging findings support this.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient has a high-risk profile for progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) due to prolonged ozanimod use (8 years), a known immunosuppressive agent, and persistent lymphopenia (nadir 330 cells/µL). PML is strongly associated with JC virus (JCV) reactivation in immunocompromised individuals.

<step 2> MRI findings of punctate gadolinium-enhancing lesions ("milky way sign") are highly suggestive of PML-IRIS (immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome), which occurs when immune recovery triggers inflammation around JCV-infected brain tissue. This aligns with elevated serum neurofilament light-chain (sNfL) levels (113.7 pg/mL), indicative of active neuroaxonal damage.

<step 3> CSF JCV PCR positivity (34 copies/mL), even transiently, supports PML diagnosis. Subsequent negative CSF JCV PCR does not exclude PML, as viral shedding can fluctuate or diminish during IRIS. Normal CSF cytobiochemistry rules out other infectious/inflammatory mimics.

<step 4> Persistent lymphopenia and lack of overt neurological deficits despite imaging abnormalities suggest atypical PML-IRIS presentation, possibly due to partial immune suppression from ozanimod. Worsening mentation history further supports CNS involvement.

<step 5> Alternative diagnoses (e.g., MS relapse, COVID-19 complications) are unlikely given the absence of typical MS lesion features on MRI, normal immune globulins, negative HIV, and temporal disconnect from SARS-CoV-2 infection.

### Answer:
PML-IRIS in the context of ozanimod therapy for multiple sclerosis.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient has a high-risk profile for progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) due to prolonged ozanimod use (8 years), a known immunosuppressive agent, and persistent lymphopenia (nadir 330 cells/µL). PML is strongly associated with JC virus (JCV) reactivation in immunocompromised individuals.

MRI findings of punctate gadolinium-enhancing lesions ("milky way sign") are highly suggestive of PML-IRIS (immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome), which occurs when immune recovery triggers inflammation around JCV-infected brain tissue. This aligns with elevated serum neurofilament light-chain (sNfL) levels (113.7 pg/mL), indicative of active neuroaxonal damage.

CSF JCV PCR positivity (34 copies/mL), even transiently, supports PML diagnosis. Subsequent negative CSF JCV PCR does not exclude PML, as viral shedding can fluctuate or diminish during IRIS. Normal CSF cytobiochemistry rules out other infectious/inflammatory mimics.

Persistent lymphopenia and lack of overt neurological deficits despite imaging abnormalities suggest atypical PML-IRIS presentation, possibly due to partial immune suppression from ozanimod. Worsening mentation history further supports CNS involvement.

Alternative diagnoses (e.g., MS relapse, COVID-19 complications) are unlikely given the absence of typical MS lesion features on MRI, normal immune globulins, negative HIV, and temporal disconnect from SARS-CoV-2 infection.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1 Considered multiple sclerosis (MS) activity flare-up but excluded based on the absence of typical MRI findings for MS flares and abnormally high sNfL levels.

Step 2 Strongly suspected progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) due to MRI findings like the "milky way sign," lymphopenia, and extreme elevation of sNfL; however, definitive diagnosis was limited by the negative JCV PCR in CSF.

Step 3 Favored PML with immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (PML-IRIS) due to the absence of new gadolinium-enhancing lesions in follow-up MRIs, presence of punctate gadolinium enhancements surrounding lesions (hallmark of IRIS), and continuing ozanimod treatment possibly leading to partial immune reconstitution; SARS-CoV-2 infection months prior may also have contributed.
Step 4 Lesser likelihood of other opportunistic infections or malignancies due to normal metabolic and immune workup, MRI findings specific to PML, and absence of systemic symptoms.
Step 5 Excluded alternative CNS conditions like vasculitis or other white matter diseases based on MRI, CSF findings, and the clinical course being inconsistent with these conditions.
Step 6 Final diagnosis: "Possible progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy with immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (PML-IRIS)" in a patient treated with ozanimod for multiple sclerosis.
